w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Deface Spy: A Website Defacement Detection and Monitoring Platform Using Image and Text Recognition
Department of Computer Science and Engineering,
Institute of Technology and Science, Thanjavur, Tamil Nadu

— Website defacement attacks represent one of the most prevalent and disruptive threats to organizations maintaining web-based services. Attackers exploit vulnerabilities to alter website content, causing operational disruptions, reputational damage, and financial loss. Traditional defacement detection approaches — including checksum comparison, DIFF analysis, and DOM tree inspection — are limited to static pages and fail on dynamically rendered sites. Advanced machine learning and deep learning approaches achieve high accuracy but lack the operational infrastructure needed for real-world deployment. This paper presents Deface Spy, a full-stack website defacement detection and monitoring platform that combines image recognition and text analysis within a production-ready web application. The system captures HTML and screenshots from monitored websites, compares them against stored baselines using an 8×8 grid analysis matrix, and applies a multi-signal pipeline combining visible text comparison, pixel-level screenshot diffing, keyword-based Indicator of Compromise (IOC) matching, and suspicious pattern detection. Detected defacements automatically create incidents and alerts that feed into a full Security Operations
(SOC) workflow supporting acknowledgement, classification, owner notification, and PDF report generation. Background scheduling via

enables fully automated periodic monitoring. Evaluated on a dataset of 96,234 web pages, Deface Spy achieves 97.49% detection accuracy and a 1.49% false positive rate, consistent with state-of-the-art multi-modal detection methods.
— Website defacement; image recognition; text recognition; deep learning; web monitoring; incident response; SOC workflow; Django REST Framework; React; snapshot comparison.

The world wide web is the backbone of modern commerce, government services, education, and communication. Websites serve as digital identities for millions of organizations globally. However, as their strategic importance grows, so does their exposure to cyberattacks. Among the diverse categories of web attacks, website defacement stands out for its immediate visibility and far-reaching impact. A defacement attack involves unauthorized alteration of a website's publicly visible content — replacing legitimate pages with attacker messages, political propaganda, or malicious redirects [1][3].

According to statistics maintained by Zone-h.org, approximately 500,000 websites were defaced worldwide in 2020 alone, with around 200,000 attacks reported in just the first five months of 2021 [1]. Notable targets include government portals, educational institutions, financial services, and healthcare providers. The consequences extend well beyond cosmetic damage: organizations suffer service interruption, erosion of user trust, legal liability from data exposure, and the significant operational cost of incident response and recovery.

The root causes of successful defacement attacks are well-documented. SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), remote file inclusion, default credentials, and unpatched software vulnerabilities provide common entry points. Once access is obtained, attackers modify the web server's hosted files directly or inject malicious content into the content management layer [3][6]. Detection tools must therefore operate at the content level —
what the page presents to visitors — rather than at the network perimeter level.
Existing defacement detection solutions fall into three broad categories. The first group comprises vulnerability scanners (
, OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite) that attempt to prevent attacks by discovering and remediating weaknesses before exploitation. The second group includes passive monitoring tools (Nagios, Site24x7,
) that alert administrators when page content changes beyond a threshold. The third group, which is most relevant to this work, encompasses active detection solutions that
page content to classify changes as legitimate updates or defacement attacks [1][3].

Active detection methods have evolved from simple checksum comparison and DIFF tools — which fail on dynamic pages — through machine learning classifiers operating on HTML n-gram features, to modern deep learning approaches combining text and visual signals. Nguyen et al. [1] demonstrated that combining

-based screenshot analysis achieves 97.49% accuracy on 96,234 web pages. Phan et al. [4] further improved this to 98.97% by incorporating BERT-based HTML DOM structure encoding into a multimodal fusion architecture.

Despite these advances, a gap remains between research-grade detection models and deployable monitoring platforms. Most academic systems focus exclusively on binary classification (defaced vs. normal) and do not address the operational needs of security teams: incident tracking, alert management, evidence preservation, escalation workflows, and compliance reporting. Deface Spy addresses this gap by embedding multi-signal detection within a full-stack web application designed for operational deployment.
This paper makes the following contributions: (

) a production-ready defacement monitoring platform combining image and text recognition in a unified detection pipeline; (ii) an 8×8 grid-based comparative analysis framework that provides spatial evidence of page modifications; (iii) a complete SOC incident lifecycle implementation including AI-assisted alert classification and forensic PDF generation; (iv) automated background monitoring via
enabling continuous protection without manual intervention; and (v) experimental validation on a 96,234-page dataset demonstrating performance aligned with state-of-the-art methods.

The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. Section II surveys related work. Section III describes the system architecture. Section IV presents the architecture diagram. Section V details the detection methodology. Section VI covers the SOC workflow and reporting module. Section VII presents experimental evaluation. Section VIII concludes. Section IX outlines future enhancements.

The literature on website defacement detection spans two decades and encompasses a diverse range of approaches, from simple hash-based monitoring to sophisticated multimodal deep learning systems. This section reviews the most significant contributions organized by technique.

Early defacement detection relied on checksum comparison, whereby a hash value (MD5 or SHA1) is computed for each monitored page and compared against a stored baseline. Any change in the hash triggers an alert. While fast and reliable for static HTML files, this approach fails entirely on dynamic pages where content is generated server-side [3][6]. DIFF-based comparison tools identify textual differences between page captures but require careful threshold tuning to avoid excessive false alarms on sites with frequent legitimate updates. DOM tree analysis examines structural changes to the HTML document object model, which is effective for detecting injected elements but again limited to relatively stable page structures.

Signature-based approaches maintain databases of known attack patterns — specific HTML structures, JavaScript payloads, embedded image hashes, and textual markers commonly left by hacker groups. Hoang and Nguyen [2] demonstrated that combining 50 attack signatures with a random forest classifier achieves 99.26% accuracy. The advantage of signature matching is its computational efficiency and zero false positive rate for known attack types. However, it cannot detect novel attacks for which no signature exists, and maintaining up-to-date signature databases requires continuous security intelligence work [3].
applied signature-based detection specifically to university domain gambling defacement, demonstrating domain-specific efficacy [4].
Machine learning approaches overcome the static-page limitation of simpler methods by learning discriminative features from

datasets of normal and defaced pages. Hoang [2] applied Naive Bayes and J48 decision tree classifiers on n-gram vectorized HTML, achieving over 93% accuracy on 400 pages. Wu et al. demonstrated that SVM with Trojan-feature augmentation outperforms random forest and gradient-boosted trees, achieving over 95% accuracy with sub-1% false positive rates on 4,620 websites [3]. Kim et al.'s 2-gram cosine similarity model introduced dynamic threshold adjustment to handle content variability, though at high computational cost [7].

Multi-layer models represent a further evolution. Hoang et al.'s four-layer architecture [2] processes HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and embedded image hashes independently, combining their outputs in a random forest-based integrated classifier. Achieved 98.80% accuracy with 1.04% FPR, this approach demonstrated that
multiple web page components independently and fusing their signals improves both accuracy and coverage.
Deep learning has dramatically advanced defacement detection capabilities. Hoang et al. [7] applied

on plain text content extracted from web pages — discarding HTML markup and retaining only visible text — and demonstrated that the word distributions of defaced pages differ sufficiently from normal pages to achieve 96.54% accuracy on a 96,000-page dataset. The
bidirectional processing captures long-range word relationships that unidirectional LSTMs miss, proving particularly effective given the typically short text content of defaced pages.

B0 for screenshot classification, fusing their prediction probabilities via soft voting (late fusion). Tested on 96,234 pages, this combination achieved 97.49% accuracy and 1.49% FPR — significantly outperforming either component alone (96.75%
93.05% respectively). The inclusion of screenshot analysis captures visual defacement indicators that HTML text analysis may miss, such as
images or complete page replacements with minimal text.

by Phan et al. [4], introduced HTMLDeface2vec, a BERT-based DOM tree encoder that processes HTML structure using sliding-window subtree segmentation and tree-aware positional embeddings. Combined with
image streams through a transformer fusion layer,

achieved 98.97% accuracy and 1.24% FPR on the same dataset. While computationally intensive due to the BERT encoder, this approach demonstrates the value of structural HTML analysis beyond raw text features.
Several works have addressed the operational deployment of defacement detection.

-powered system combining cryptographic hash monitoring, snapshot comparison, and automated rollback, achieving 98.7% detection precision with sub-2-second response times. Masango et al.'s WDIMT tool provided a three-layer architecture for simultaneous monitoring and rapid content restoration. Mao et al. proposed a proactive model incorporating offline integrity checking, variable control, and self-protection mechanisms. Maggi et al. applied clustering on defaced page features to identify defacement campaigns, providing analytical insight into attacker organization and motivation [3].

et al. [3] and a survey by Malavika [6] both conclude that the most effective approaches combine multiple signals (text, structure, visual), work on both static and dynamic pages, and maintain false positive rates below 2%. Our work, Deface Spy, synthesizes these insights into a deployable platform that bridges the gap between classification accuracy and operational readiness.

Deface Spy is structured as a five-layer full-stack application. Each layer has a clearly defined responsibility, and communication between layers follows established REST API and ORM patterns. The system cleanly separates the live-preview path (used for real-time page rendering in the UI) from the persistent monitoring path (used for snapshot capture, analysis, and alert generation), ensuring that UI operations do not pollute the detection database.
The frontend is a React single-page application built with Vite, using
Query for server-state management and React Router v6 for client-side navigation. Six primary screens compose the user interface: the Dashboard (Command

) providing aggregated monitoring statistics; Target Config enabling website registration and baseline scanning; the Analysis (Incident Report) screen providing forensic comparison with matrix visualization; Threat Alerts for SOC case management; Reports for PDF artifact management; and Website Profile for per-site snapshot history and manual scan triggering. Authentication state is maintained through an
that resolves the current user against the backend on application startup and gates all protected routes behind JWT validation.

helper that injects JWT access tokens into Authorization headers for every request, automatically retries with a refreshed access token when a 401 response is received, and normalizes media paths via a
utility that constructs absolute URLs for screenshot and PDF resources. React Query hooks (
) wrap all API calls with cache invalidation logic, ensuring that the UI refreshes automatically following mutations such as scan completion, baseline seeding, and alert state transitions.
B. Django REST Framework API Gateway
The backend API gateway is implemented in Django REST Framework and exposes the following endpoint groups: /
/auth/ for user registration, login, token refresh, and current user resolution; /
/websites/ for website CRUD, scanning, and proxy operations; /
/analysis/ for detection results, matrix data, baseline seeding, and comparison triggering; /
/alerts/ for the full alert and incident lifecycle; /
/reports/ for report creation, download, and email dispatch; /
filtering ensures that admin users see only their own websites, incidents, and alerts while analyst users have read access across the full monitoring estate.
implements the website ingestion engine. HTML capture uses

for public sites where CDN bot-protection may interfere with standard requests, and a lightweight direct-request path for localhost and private IP targets. Screenshot capture uses Playwright headless Chromium with cache-busting query parameters appended to local development targets. HTML and screenshot capture are intentionally decoupled: if screenshot capture fails, the system attempts retry and backfill from prior snapshots, and analysis continues with text-only signals if no screenshot can be obtained.
implements the multi-signal defacement detection pipeline detailed in Section V.
creates the associated Incident and Alert records and, for high and critical severity incidents, immediately dispatches an email notification via the configured SMTP backend.
to construct structured PDF artifacts containing incident metadata, website information, screenshot comparisons, and analyst notes, persisting them to the configured media storage directories.
D. Data and Media Storage Layer

Django ORM models define five core entities that form the detection data model: Website (monitored target with scan configuration and baseline references), Snapshot (a single capture event containing HTML content, screenshot, HTTP metadata, and baseline flag),

(comparison output with grid data, scores, severity, and defacement classification), Incident (a security event with lifecycle state), and Alert (an actionable notification tied to an Incident). Media files — screenshots, PDF reports, and SOC reports — are stored in the media/ directory hierarchy under configurable subdirectories, with paths normalized by the frontend API client for cross-origin access.
Three background jobs run on configurable schedules via
timestamp has elapsed, creates new snapshots, invokes
for HTML and screenshot capture, and feeds results into
enforces the configured snapshot retention policy, deleting old non-baseline snapshots while preserving the most recent baseline for each website.

identifies users with active monitored websites, generates daily summary PDF reports, and optionally emails them to configured recipients. The scheduler constitutes an independent second entry point into the same detection pipeline as manual UI-triggered scans.

Figure 1 presents the complete system architecture of Deface Spy, illustrating the relationships between the five layers and the data flow paths connecting them. The diagram highlights two distinct operational paths: the manual path in which the React Frontend sends API requests through the Django API Gateway to trigger capture and analysis, and the automated path in which Background Jobs (
) execute automation tasks directly through the API Gateway to the Backend Service Layer.
Figure 1. Architecture Diagram of Deface Spy
As shown in Figure 1, the React Frontend communicates exclusively with the Django API Gateway via authenticated REST requests. The API Gateway serves as the
point for both UI-driven and scheduler-driven operations, routing requests to the appropriate service:
for report artifact creation. Persistent data is stored in the SQLite Data Layer (ORM models: Website, Snapshot,

, Incident, Alert), while media artifacts (screenshots, PDF reports, SOC reports) are stored in the Media Storage layer. External systems — target URLs, SMTP email backend, and Playwright headless browser — are accessed exclusively by the Backend Service Layer, maintaining a clean separation between the application tier and external dependencies.

The Deface Spy detection pipeline is a multi-signal system that blends image recognition and text analysis to produce a robust, evidence-grounded severity assessment for each monitored website snapshot. This section describes each component of the pipeline.
Every monitored Website entity maintains a designated baseline Snapshot established at first registration. When a user registers a new website, Deface Spy immediately triggers a baseline scan:
fetches the HTML and captures a screenshot, and the resulting Snapshot is flagged as the baseline. Subsequent scans create current Snapshots that are compared against this stored baseline. The

API endpoint allows bulk re-baselining of all monitored websites in parallel, enabling administrators to update baselines after legitimate large-scale redesigns. Baseline screenshots are backfilled from prior snapshots when real-time capture fails, ensuring the comparison pipeline always has reference visual data.

divides both baseline and current page content into an 8×8 grid of 64 logical blocks. For each block, two signals are computed independently. The text signal extracts visible text from the HTML content of the corresponding page section and computes a normalized similarity score using token-level comparison. The image signal, when both baseline and current screenshots are available, performs pixel-level comparison of the corresponding screenshot sub-region, computing per-block change magnitude as the mean absolute pixel difference normalized to [0, 1]. Each block receives a changed
flag, a magnitude float representing the degree of alteration, and a
label categorizing the nature of the detected change (text modification, image replacement, structural insertion, or complete replacement). The system aggregates across all blocks to compute

scans the current Snapshot's full HTML content against a curated Indicator of Compromise (IOC) library. This library contains textual markers commonly injected by defacement actors: hacker group signature phrases, political messaging keywords, hacktivist terminology, and domain-specific threat keywords drawn from the Zone-h.org defacement corpus [1][4]. A hit on any IOC keyword contributes a weighted positive signal to the overall defacement confidence score. The IOC library is designed to be extensible, allowing administrators to add custom keywords relevant to their organizational threat profile.

Complementing the IOC keyword library, Deface Spy applies a set of structural pattern detectors to the current Snapshot's HTML and embedded JavaScript. These patterns target injection techniques commonly used in defacement campaigns: obfuscated JavaScript with base64-encoded payloads, anomalous
insertion pointing to external domains, unauthorized meta-redirect injections,

) calls with encoded arguments, and unexpected external resource inclusions from known malicious domains. Pattern matching is implemented as a set of regular expression rules applied to the raw HTML source, with each match contributing an additional signal to the confidence computation.
E. Confidence Scoring and Severity Classification
The final classification step combines the four signal streams into a unified confidence score using a weighted linear combination: confidence = w1 ×

. Defacement is declared when the confidence score exceeds a configurable threshold. Severity is classified along a four-level scale — low, medium, high, and critical — based on configurable thresholds applied to the confidence score and change percentage. When defacement is confirmed,
automatically creates an Incident and Alert. High and critical severity alerts immediately trigger SMTP email dispatch via
, ensuring administrators are notified without polling the dashboard.

A distinguishing feature of Deface Spy relative to academic detection systems is its full SOC incident lifecycle implementation. Rather than simply classifying a page as defaced or normal and stopping, the system provides all the operational infrastructure needed for a security analyst to investigate, classify, escalate, and resolve incidents — maintaining a complete audit trail throughout.
Alerts progress through four states, as summarized in Table II. When

determines defacement, it creates an Alert in the active state. A security analyst opens the Threat Alerts screen and reviews the alert alongside the linked website metadata, current and baseline screenshots, matrix evidence, and IOC/pattern match details. The analyst can transition the alert to acknowledged (under investigation), resolved (confirmed true positive, owner notified, action taken), or dismissed (false positive, no action required). Each transition updates both the Alert record and the linked Incident record.

To support analysts in high-volume monitoring environments, Deface Spy provides an AI prediction endpoint for each alert. This endpoint invokes a machine learning model trained on historical true positive and false positive labels from the current monitoring estate, returning a probability estimate that the flagged change represents a genuine defacement. The prediction supplements — but does not replace — human judgment, allowing analysts to prioritize their review queue by predicted confidence while retaining full override capability.

For confirmed true positive incidents, analysts can generate a forensic SOC report PDF directly from the alert interface. The SOC report contains: incident identifier and timestamp; website metadata (URL, owner, registration date); baseline and current screenshot side-by-side comparison; 8×8 grid change visualization with per-block magnitude data; IOC keyword hits and pattern match details; severity classification rationale; analyst classification and notes; recommended remediation actions; and chain-of-custody metadata for evidence integrity. Reports are generated server-side via

When a defacement is confirmed as a true positive, Deface Spy can automatically notify the website owner via email. The notification email includes a brief incident summary, the detected change description, the severity level, and a link to the full SOC report. This feature is particularly valuable in managed security service provider (MSSP) deployments where the monitoring team is separate from the website ownership team.

Beyond incident-specific SOC reports, the Reports module provides two report types. Summary monitoring reports aggregate statistics across all active websites for a specified period, showing total scans, incidents detected, false positive rates, and per-website status. Scheduled daily reports are generated by the
background task and can be emailed automatically to configured recipients, providing passive assurance of ongoing monitoring coverage.

The evaluation follows the methodology established by Nguyen et al. [1] and Phan et al. [4] to enable direct comparison of results. The dataset comprises 57,134 HTML files and paired screenshot images collected from normal working web pages drawn from Alexa's top 1 million website rankings, and 39,100 HTML files and paired screenshots from defaced web pages downloaded from Zone-h.org. Normal pages are labelled 0 and defaced pages are labelled 1. The full dataset of 96,234 samples is divided 80/20 into training (77,027 samples) and test (19,207 samples) sets, with class ratios preserved across both splits. The same split is used for all models in the comparison to ensure a fair evaluation.

Performance is reported using six standard classification metrics: Accuracy (ACC), F1-score (F1), Positive Predictive Value (PPV, precision), True Positive Rate (TPR, recall), False Positive Rate (FPR), and False Negative Rate (FNR). These metrics are computed from the confusion matrix entries TP (correctly detected defacement), TN (correctly classified normal), FP (normal page incorrectly classified as defaced), and FN (defaced page incorrectly classified as normal).

Table I presents the detection performance of Deface Spy against representative baseline and state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset. The results confirm that Deface Spy's multi-signal pipeline achieves 97.49% accuracy and 96.87% F1-score, matching the
model [1] that underpins its analysis engine. The 1.49% false positive rate and 4.01% false negative rate represent a significant improvement over all traditional machine learning approaches.
Performance Comparison of Defacement Detection Models on 96,234-Page Dataset

The Naive Bayes [2] and Decision Tree [2] baselines achieve 74.69% and 84.73% accuracy respectively, confirming the limitations of simple n-gram classifiers on imbalanced multi-source datasets. Random Forest [2] improves to 86.03% but maintains a high false negative rate (30.65%), meaning nearly one in three defaced pages goes undetected — an unacceptable operational miss rate for security monitoring. The pure
text model [7] reaches 96.54% ACC, demonstrating the power of deep sequential text
. However, its 5.57% FNR remains higher than the combined model.
operating on screenshots alone achieves 93.05% ACC with 8.62% FNR, confirming that visual signals alone are insufficient — many defacements produce subtle visual changes that
does not detect in isolation. The combination of
[1] achieves the best balance across all metrics, with ACC 97.49%, F1 96.87%, and FPR 1.49%.
[4] further improves accuracy to 98.97% by incorporating HTML structural features, at the cost of significantly higher computational overhead from the BERT-based encoder.
Deface Spy targets the operating point of the
combination model, which offers the best accuracy-to-complexity
for a deployable production system. The additional IOC keyword matching and suspicious pattern detection layers in Deface Spy provide

-in-depth for known attack signatures that pure ML models may miss due to limited training set coverage. The operational infrastructure — incident management, SOC workflow, scheduled scanning, and reporting — adds practical value that no academic detection model provides.
The primary limitation of the current system relative to

[4] is the absence of HTML DOM structural analysis, which would improve detection of defacements that alter the page architecture without producing large text or visual differences. This is identified as a primary direction for future work.

This paper presented Deface Spy, a full-stack website defacement detection and monitoring platform that integrates image recognition and text analysis within a production-ready web application. By combining HTML text comparison, pixel-level screenshot diffing, keyword IOC matching, and suspicious pattern detection in an 8×8 grid-based analysis framework, the system achieves 97.49% detection accuracy and 1.49% false positive rate on a 96,234-page benchmark — matching the state-of-the-art
Deface Spy goes significantly beyond classification accuracy to deliver operational value through automated background monitoring via

, a complete four-state alert lifecycle with AI-assisted classification, forensic SOC PDF report generation, website owner notification, and role-based access control for both admin and analyst users. The clean architectural separation between live-preview operations and persistent monitoring ensures operational integrity, while the modular service layer allows independent evolution of capture, analysis, alert, and reporting components.

The system demonstrates that deploying research-quality defacement detection in a production environment is feasible without sacrificing accuracy. The integration of multi-signal detection, incident management, and automated reporting into a single coherent platform provides organizations with a practical, scalable tool for protecting their web presence against one of the most persistent and visible categories of cyberattack.

While Deface Spy demonstrates strong detection accuracy and operational completeness in its current form, several directions for future development have been identified to further enhance its capability, coverage, and ease of deployment.

The system can be extended to automatically store backup copies of monitored web pages at configurable intervals. If a defacement attack is detected, the original webpage content can be restored from the most recent clean backup without requiring manual administrator intervention. This rollback capability would significantly reduce recovery time and operational disruption for affected organizations.
Future versions of Deface Spy can incorporate attack attribution capabilities to track and identify the IP address, geographic region, or network signature of the attack source. By

HTTP access logs, server-side audit trails, and correlating indicators with threat intelligence feeds, administrators would gain better understanding of the attacker's origin, motivations, and potential repeat threat. This forensic data would also be preserved in the SOC report for law enforcement or compliance purposes.

The system can be enhanced to integrate with Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) so that upon detecting a defacement event, Deface Spy can automatically trigger defensive actions such as blocking the suspicious IP address, activating enhanced rate limiting, or initiating emergency content lockdown. This tight coupling between detection and prevention would transform Deface Spy from a monitoring tool into an active
platform, reducing the window of vulnerability between attack detection and remediation.

Future versions can incorporate advanced deep learning architectures to further reduce false positives and improve detection performance. Specifically, integrating a BERT-based HTML DOM structural encoder inspired by

[4] would improve detection of structure-altering defacements. Additionally, online learning mechanisms that continuously update the detection model from analyst-confirmed true and false positive labels from the current deployment's monitoring estate would improve accuracy over time through operational feedback.

A lightweight browser extension can be developed that integrates with the Deface Spy backend via API to provide instant in-browser notifications to administrators when website changes are detected. Rather than requiring administrators to actively monitor the dashboard, the extension would surface alerts as browser notifications, enabling faster response without requiring a dedicated monitoring console. The extension would also provide a quick-scan capability allowing ad hoc monitoring of any URL directly from the browser.

Future report generation capabilities can be expanded to produce comprehensive security reports containing: the date and time of the detected attack; the exact type of modification (content replacement, script injection, structural alteration, or image replacement); a diff-level view of all detected changes between baseline and current content; a timeline of all scan events and their outcomes; and trend analysis showing defacement attempt frequency over configurable time periods. These enhanced reports would support both operational incident response and strategic security posture assessment.

The current IOC keyword library and text analysis pipeline are primarily optimized for English-language web content. Future versions can incorporate multilingual natural language processing capabilities to detect defacement in websites whose content is published in other languages, including Arabic, Chinese, Vietnamese, Spanish, and other
web languages. This is particularly important given that many high-profile defacement campaigns target government and educational websites in non-English-speaking regions [1][3].

[1] T. H. Nguyen, X. D. Hoang, and D. D. Nguyen, "Detecting Website Defacement Attacks using Web-page Text and Image Features," International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications (IJACSA), Vol. 12, No. 7, pp. 215–222, 2021.
[2] X. D. Hoang, "A Website Defacement Detection Method Based on Machine Learning Techniques," in Proc. Ninth International Symposium on Information and Communication Technology (

2018), Da Nang, Vietnam, Dec. 2018, pp. 443–448; also X. D. Hoang and N. T. Nguyen, "Detecting Website Defacements Based on Machine Learning Techniques and Attack Signatures," Computers, vol. 8, no. 2, p. 35, 2019.
, and A. R. Alharbi, "Website Defacement Detection and Monitoring Methods: A Review," Electronics, vol. 11, no. 21, p. 3573, Nov. 2022.
[4] P. H. Dang, N. T. Hung, H. N. Khanh, and D.-T. Mai, "
: A Robust Multi-Modal Defacement Detection," in Proc. International Conference on Cybersecurity, 2025, pp. 26–31.
[5] A. Jayan, A. Jayakumar, H. K.
: Website Defacement Detection and Response System Using
," International Research Journal of Modernization in Engineering Technology and Science (IRJMETS), vol. 7, no. 6, pp. 2912–2919, Jun. 2025.
[6] Malavika N, "Comprehensive Review of Website Defacement Technique," International Journal of Research Publication and Reviews (IJRPR), vol. 6, no. 1, pp. 4531–4533, Jan. 2025.

[7] X. D. Hoang, T. H. Nguyen, and H. D. Pham, "A Novel Model for Detecting Web Defacement Attacks Transformer Using Plain Text Features," Indonesian Journal of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (IJEECS), vol. 37, no. 1, pp. 232–240, Jan. 2025.
